Skip to content Overview Quick start Humans & agents Schema architecture Conformance Interoperability Stability & 1.0 Migration Interactive samples Root schema criterion dated-note entity-ref example extends extensions link presentation relationship rich-text status system-info token-overrides use-cases chunk component foundation guide pattern theme token accessibility anatomy api checklist content design-specifications document-blocks guidelines imports interactions motion principles scale sections states steps variants aliases category doc-origin governance last-updated links metadata preview since status summary tags thumbnail
What is DSDS?

DSDS (Design System Documentation Spec) is a JSON format for documenting design systems. It puts every piece of docs — components, tokens, themes, foundations, patterns — in a machine-readable shape.

Key idea: DSDS documents the how and why of your design system — not the token values themselves. It complements the W3C Design Tokens Format which handles the what.

 What you get
  • Structured — every section has a defined shape, no guessing
  • Machine-readable — tools can parse, generate, validate, and transform it
  • Portable — not locked to any docs tool or platform
  • Extensible — add vendor metadata without breaking compatibility
  • Validatable — JSON Schema catches errors before they reach consumers
Document structure

A DSDS file requires dsdsVersion and one of two shapes:

Single-entity files

Use entity when each component, token, or pattern lives in its own file. The kind field on the entity identifies its type.

{ "$schema": "https://designsystemdocspec.org/v0.15.1/dsds.bundled.schema.json", "dsdsVersion": "0.15.1", "entity": { "kind": "component", "identifier": "button", "name": "Button", "description": "An interactive element that triggers an action.", "metadata": { "status": "stable" }, "documentBlocks": [] } } Multi-entity files

Use entityGroups to put several entities in one file, or as a manifest that points to separate entity files.

{ "dsdsVersion": "0.15.1", "entityGroups": [ { "name": "My Design System", "entities": [ { "kind": "foundation", "identifier": "spacing", "...": "" }, { "kind": "token-group", "identifier": "color-palette", "...": "" }, { "kind": "theme", "identifier": "dark", "...": "" }, { "kind": "component", "identifier": "button", "...": "" } ] } ] }
  • dsdsVersion — the spec version this document targets; "0.15.1" for this release
  • entityGroups — array of named groups
  • entities — one array per group; mix kinds freely in display order. Each entity's kind says what type it is
Linking files with $ref

Entity arrays accept $ref objects that point to other DSDS files. This lets you keep entities in their own files and pull them together in a manifest:

{ "dsdsVersion": "0.15.1", "entityGroups": [ { "name": "Acme Design System", "entities": [ { "$ref": "./button.dsds.json#/entity" }, { "$ref": "./link.dsds.json#/entity" } ] } ] }

The #/entity fragment is a JSON Pointer. It targets the entity value in the linked file. Tools resolve $ref objects before they validate, with a library like json-schema-ref-parser.

Tip: Use single-entity files plus a $ref manifest for large systems where a different team owns each component. Use one multi-entity file for smaller systems, or when you want everything in one place.
Entity types

Every entity has a kind field that tells tools what type of thing it is. There are eight kinds.

KindCodeDescription
component"component"A reusable UI element — buttons, inputs, modals. Supports anatomy, API, variants, states, and design specs.
token"token"A single design token — color, spacing, typography. Carries type, category, and usage rules. Link to W3C DTCG definitions via source.
token-group"token-group"A nested group of related tokens. Recursive — groups can contain groups.
theme"theme"A named set of token overrides — dark mode, high-contrast, compact density, brand variants. It lists which token names change; the DTCG file holds the values.
foundation"foundation"A design base — color, typography, spacing, elevation. Carries principles, scales, and motion definitions.
pattern"pattern"A broad action pattern — nav, error messaging, empty states. It shows how components combine to solve a user need.
guide"guide"A long-form, reading-oriented document — getting-started, contribution, tutorial, migration. It carries narrative sections and step-by-step procedures.
chunk"chunk"A pre-composed block of code that captures a pattern — a copy-paste starting point built from the system's components. It requires code and declares what it composes via relationships.
 Common properties

Every entity shares the same envelope: kind, identifier, name, optional description and metadata, plus documentBlocks and agentDocumentBlocks. See Common entity properties for the full table, including the token exceptions.

The optional agentDocumentBlocks array is a separate space for agent-only docs. It holds generation limits with evidence and notes that tell apart similar entities. It uses the same block kinds as documentBlocks. Tools never show it to humans; agents read both arrays.

Status: string or object

For the simple case, status is a string in metadata:

"metadata": { "status": "stable" }

When you need per-platform tracking:

"metadata": { "status": { "overall": "stable", "platforms": { "react": { "status": "stable", "since": "1.0.0" }, "ios": { "status": "experimental", "since": "3.0.0" } } } }
The document block system

Structured docs live in the documentBlocks array on each entity. Each document block entry is typed by its kind field.

KindApplies ToDescription
accessibilityallSpecialized, structured accessibility documentation for an artifact.
checklistallAn explicit checklist for an artifact: a list of concrete items an agent (or a person) works through when building, reviewing, or integrating it.
contentallSpecialized, structured content documentation: a terminology dictionary of recommended labels and per-concern localization entries.
guidelinesallActionable usage rules for an artifact.
sectionsallA block of narrative documentation made up of one or more titled sections.
use-casesallWhen to use an artifact and when to choose something else.
importscomponent, guideDocuments how to import a component across platforms.
anatomycomponent, patternDocuments the visual structure of a component or pattern by listing its named sub-elements (parts).
statescomponent, patternDocuments all interactive states of a component or pattern.
variantscomponent, patternDocuments all dimensions of visual or behavioral variation on a component or pattern.
apicomponentDocuments the code-level interface of a component on a single platform.
design-specificationscomponentDocuments the baseline measurable visual specs of a component: the default design properties, spacing relationships, dimension limits, typographic settings, and responsive behavior.
motionfoundationDocuments the motion system for a foundation: named easing curves, timing functions, recommended durations, and usage guidance.
principlesfoundationDocuments guiding principles for a foundation or design domain.
scalefoundationDocuments an ordered scale: a deliberate progression of values that limits design decisions to a harmonious, consistent set.
stepsguideA procedure made up of an ordered or unordered sequence of steps.
interactionspatternDocuments the interaction flow of a pattern as an ordered sequence of steps.
Use cases: when to use it

The use-cases block lists scenarios. An optional purpose statement gives the broad answer. Each item has a stance: "recommended" (do use) or "discouraged" (don't use, with an alternative):

{ "kind": "use-cases", "purpose": "Buttons trigger immediate actions within a surface.", "items": [ { "description": "When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form.", "stance": "recommended" }, { "description": "When the action navigates to a different page.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "link", "rationale": "Links carry native navigation semantics." } } ] } Guidelines: how to use it

Each guideline pairs a guidance statement with a rationale and an RFC 2119 level:

{ "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Limit each surface to one primary button.", "rationale": "Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy.", "level": "must", "category": "visual-design" } ] }

The level field is named for RFC 2119. Its values are lowercase kebab-case like every DSDS vocabulary — "must", "should", "should-not", "must-not" — and tools display them as badges: MUST, SHOULD, SHOULD NOT, MUST NOT. Agents treat must/must-not entries as hard limits. Optional evidence records the backing, and criteria define testable success conditions that checks can run against.

Criteria: how to verify it

A criterion turns a guideline into something checkable. Each one pairs a stable identifier with a testable statement. Test runs report pass or fail against the identifier.

The verification field says how a result is produced:

  • automated — a runner decides the outcome. A check names the tool (its scheme) and carries that tool's config. Required for this mode.
  • assisted — a tool surfaces candidates; a human decides.
  • manual — pure human judgment. No check applies; techniques and failures act as the reviewer's procedure.

When verification is omitted, tools must not assume the criterion is automatable. The set is closed — it answers who decides. A named procedure such as a design review is the how; put it in techniques on a manual criterion.

examples on a criterion are test cases. Each declares an outcome — "pass" or "fail" — so a runner can confirm the check still detects what it claims to:

{ "identifier": "icon-button-name-present", "statement": "Every icon-only button exposes a non-empty accessible name.", "level": "must", "verification": "automated", "check": { "scheme": "axe-core", "rule": "button-name" }, "examples": [ { "title": "Labelled icon button", "outcome": "pass", "presentation": { "kind": "code", "code": "", "language": "jsx" } }, { "title": "Unlabelled icon button", "outcome": "fail", "presentation": { "kind": "code", "code": "", "language": "jsx" } } ] }

The statement is always the source of truth. The check is its executable shadow — execution semantics belong to the named tool, not to DSDS.

For humans and agents

A DSDS document has two readers: people and agents. documentBlocks is the default home — the docs a person reads and acts on. Agents read it too.

agentDocumentBlocks is an optional, agent-only space. Put firm, ready-to-act notes there — the kind that would be clutter for people. Examples: hard MUST / MUST NOT rules, notes that tell look-alikes apart, and checks an agent can run.

Tools never show agentDocumentBlocks to people, and agents read both arrays. For when to use each, see DSDS for humans and agents.

Conventions and traps

Four conventions catch most first-time authors. Knowing them up front saves a validation round-trip.

status has exactly two shapes. A bare string, or an object with overall. There is no value property:

"status": "stable" "status": { "overall": "stable", "platforms": { "react": { "status": "stable" } } }

It's always identifier, never name, for machine keys. API properties, anatomy parts, criteria, variants — the machine-readable key is identifier everywhere. name is only ever a display name.

tokens is a purpose-keyed map, not an array. Keys say what the token controls; values name the token:

"tokens": { "text-color": "color-button-fg", "background": "color-button-bg" }

Flag vs. enum variants. A boolean on/off dimension is a flag variant. A set of named options is an enum variant — and a single-entry enum is valid (dimensions often ship one value at a time).

Minimal examples

These are the smallest valid entities — the bare minimum. Copy one, fill in your content, and add document block entries as you go.

Component

A component needs kind, identifier, name, and description. Everything else is optional.

{ "kind": "component", "identifier": "button", "name": "Button", "description": "A clickable element that triggers an action.", "metadata": { "status": "stable", "category": "action", "tags": [ "action", "submit", "click", "CTA", "trigger", "call-to-action", "interactive" ] }, "documentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "items": [ { "description": "When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form or confirming a dialog.", "stance": "recommended" }, { "description": "When the action is navigation to another page.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "link", "rationale": "Links communicate navigation; buttons communicate actions." } } ] } ], "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "purpose": "Trigger a user-initiated action within the current view without causing page navigation.", "items": [ { "description": "Use button for in-page actions; use link for navigation to a URL.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "link" } }, { "description": "Use button when a visible text label is present; use icon-button for icon-only affordances.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "icon-button" } } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Do not use for navigating to a different page or URL.", "level": "must-not" }, { "guidance": "Limit each surface to one primary-emphasis button.", "level": "must" }, { "guidance": "Always provide an accessible label via visible text or aria-label.", "level": "must" }, { "guidance": "Using a button to navigate to another page.", "rationale": "Instead: Use a link element with href.", "level": "must-not" }, { "guidance": "Placing multiple primary buttons on the same surface.", "rationale": "Instead: Use one primary and one or more secondary or tertiary buttons.", "level": "must-not" } ] } ] } Token

A token needs kind, identifier, and tokenType. Use the optional source property to link back to the W3C DTCG file that holds the real value.

{ "kind": "token", "identifier": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "source": { "file": "tokens/color.tokens.json", "path": "color.text.primary" } } Token group

Groups arrange tokens in a tree. The children array can contain tokens or nested groups.

{ "kind": "token-group", "identifier": "color-text", "description": "Semantic text color tokens.", "metadata": { "status": "stable", "category": "semantic", "tags": [ "token", "group", "palette", "scale", "family", "collection" ] }, "tokenType": "color", "source": { "file": "tokens/color.tokens.json", "path": "color.text" }, "children": [ { "kind": "token", "identifier": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "source": { "file": "tokens/color.tokens.json", "path": "color.text.primary" } }, { "kind": "token", "identifier": "color-text-secondary", "tokenType": "color", "source": { "file": "tokens/color.tokens.json", "path": "color.text.secondary" } }, { "kind": "token", "identifier": "color-text-disabled", "tokenType": "color", "source": { "file": "tokens/color.tokens.json", "path": "color.text.disabled" } } ], "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "sections", "items": [ { "title": "Intent", "body": "Define and organize a set of related design tokens, providing shared defaults for type and category." } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Do not reference base palette tokens directly in component code.", "rationale": "Always reference semantic tokens that point to palette values.", "level": "must-not" } ] } ] } Theme

A theme provides overrides — an array of token names that differ from the default. The DTCG file holds the real override values.

{ "kind": "theme", "identifier": "dark", "name": "Dark Mode", "description": "Dark color mode for reduced-light environments.", "metadata": { "status": "stable", "category": "color-mode", "tags": [ "dark-mode", "color-mode", "night", "low-light", "theme", "override" ] }, "overrides": [ { "token": "color-background-default", "description": "Inverted default surface for dark mode." }, { "token": "color-text-primary", "description": "High-contrast text on dark surfaces." }, { "token": "color-border-default", "description": "Subtle border visible on dark backgrounds." } ], "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "purpose": "Apply a dark color palette for low-light environments and user preference, maintaining WCAG AA contrast throughout.", "items": [ { "description": "Use dark for low-light comfort; use high-contrast for users with low vision requiring maximum contrast.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "high-contrast" } } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Always apply at the application root, not on individual components.", "level": "must" }, { "guidance": "Do not mix dark-mode and light-mode semantic tokens on the same surface.", "level": "must-not" }, { "guidance": "Applying dark theme only to part of a page while the rest remains light.", "rationale": "Instead: Use inline theme switching for isolated components; apply the theme at the root for global coverage.", "level": "must-not" } ] } ] } Foundation

A foundation documents a design base. Guidelines like principles, scale, and motion are specific to foundations.

{ "kind": "foundation", "identifier": "spacing", "name": "Spacing", "description": "The spatial system governing layout rhythm and component density.", "metadata": { "status": "stable", "tags": [ "spacing", "scale", "gap", "padding", "margin", "whitespace", "layout", "density" ] }, "documentBlocks": [ { "kind": "principles", "items": [ { "title": "Use the scale", "description": "Always pick a value from the spacing scale. Avoid arbitrary pixel values." } ] } ], "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "purpose": "Document spacing scale tokens and enforce their consistent application across layout, components, and typography.", "items": [ { "description": "Spacing governs whitespace and layout gaps; typography governs type size, weight, and line height.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "typography foundation" } } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Do not substitute spacing tokens with typography or icon-size tokens.", "level": "must-not" }, { "guidance": "Always use the next scale step up or down rather than custom values.", "level": "should" } ] } ] } Pattern

A pattern shows how components combine to solve a user need. It supports interactions for flow steps.

{ "kind": "pattern", "identifier": "form-validation", "name": "Form Validation", "description": "Validates user input on form submission and communicates errors inline.", "metadata": { "status": "stable", "tags": [ "error", "validation", "form", "feedback", "inline-error", "error-summary", "alert" ] }, "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "purpose": "Guide multi-component composition for surfacing, communicating, and resolving form validation errors.", "items": [ { "description": "Use form-validation for input errors the user can fix at the field; use alert for system or status messages not tied to a field.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "alert" } }, { "description": "Use form-validation to communicate failure; success confirmation belongs to a toast, which never carries errors.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "toast" } } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Always include both an inline field error and a summary when multiple fields fail.", "level": "must" }, { "guidance": "Do not use toast notifications as the sole error communication for form validation.", "level": "must-not" }, { "guidance": "Do not show errors only on submit with no inline field feedback.", "rationale": "Users fix problems fastest at the field. Show inline errors on blur and collect them in a summary on submit.", "level": "must-not" } ] } ], "relationships": [ { "relation": "composes", "target": "form-field", "role": "Displays inline error messages" }, { "relation": "composes", "target": "button", "role": "Triggers form submission" }, { "relation": "composes", "target": "toast", "role": "Confirms successful submission" } ] } Full document

Here's a complete, minimal DSDS file with one of each entity type:

{ "$schema": "../schema/dsds.bundled.schema.json", "dsdsVersion": "0.15.1", "systemInfo": { "name": "Minimal Example" }, "entityGroups": [ { "name": "Minimal Design System", "entities": [ { "kind": "component", "identifier": "button", "name": "Button", "description": "A clickable button for triggering actions.", "metadata": { "status": "stable", "tags": [ "action", "submit", "click", "CTA", "trigger", "call-to-action", "interactive" ] }, "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "purpose": "Trigger a user-initiated action within the current view without causing page navigation.", "items": [ { "description": "Use button for in-page actions; use link for navigation to a URL.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "link" } }, { "description": "Use button when a visible text label is present; use icon-button for icon-only affordances.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "icon-button" } } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Do not use for navigating to a different page or URL.", "level": "must-not", "rationale": "Buttons signal actions and links signal navigation. Assistive technology announces each differently, and users rely on that distinction to predict what a control will do." }, { "guidance": "Limit each surface to one primary-emphasis button.", "level": "must", "rationale": "A single primary action keeps the visual hierarchy clear. When several buttons compete at primary emphasis, the intended next step becomes ambiguous." }, { "guidance": "Always provide an accessible label via visible text or aria-label.", "level": "must", "rationale": "Icon-only buttons without an accessible name are unusable with screen readers and fail WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value)." }, { "guidance": "Using a button to navigate to another page.", "rationale": "Instead: Use a link element with href.", "level": "must-not" }, { "guidance": "Placing multiple primary buttons on the same surface.", "rationale": "Instead: Use one primary and one or more secondary or tertiary buttons.", "level": "must-not" } ] } ], "documentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "items": [ { "description": "When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form, saving data, or opening a dialog.", "stance": "recommended" }, { "description": "When the action navigates to a different page or URL — use a link instead, which carries native navigation semantics.", "stance": "discouraged" } ] } ] }, { "kind": "token-group", "identifier": "tokens", "children": [ { "kind": "token-group", "identifier": "color-text", "source": { "file": "tokens/color.tokens.json", "path": "color.text" }, "children": [ { "kind": "token", "identifier": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "source": { "file": "tokens/color.tokens.json", "path": "color.text.primary" } }, { "kind": "token", "identifier": "color-text-secondary", "tokenType": "color", "source": { "file": "tokens/color.tokens.json", "path": "color.text.secondary" } } ], "metadata": { "tags": [ "token", "group", "palette", "scale", "family", "collection" ] }, "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "sections", "items": [ { "title": "Intent", "body": "Define and organize a set of related design tokens, providing shared defaults for type and category." } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Do not reference base palette tokens directly in component code.", "rationale": "Always reference semantic tokens that point to palette values.", "level": "must-not" } ] } ] } ], "metadata": { "tags": [ "token", "group", "palette", "scale", "family", "collection" ] }, "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "sections", "items": [ { "title": "Intent", "body": "Define and organize a set of related design tokens, providing shared defaults for type and category." } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Do not reference base palette tokens directly in component code.", "rationale": "Always reference semantic tokens that point to palette values.", "level": "must-not" } ] } ] }, { "kind": "theme", "identifier": "dark", "name": "Dark Mode", "description": "Dark color mode for low-light environments.", "metadata": { "status": "stable", "tags": [ "dark-mode", "color-mode", "night", "low-light", "theme", "override" ] }, "overrides": [ { "token": "color-text-primary", "description": "High-contrast text on dark surfaces." }, { "token": "color-text-secondary", "description": "Muted text for secondary content in dark mode." } ], "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "purpose": "Apply a dark color palette for low-light environments and user preference, maintaining WCAG AA contrast throughout.", "items": [ { "description": "Use dark for low-light comfort; use high-contrast for users with low vision requiring maximum contrast.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "high-contrast" } } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Always apply at the application root, not on individual components.", "level": "must", "rationale": "Scoping the theme to the root keeps every surface consistent. Per-component theming produces mixed-mode screens and doubles the testing surface." }, { "guidance": "Do not mix dark-mode and light-mode semantic tokens on the same surface.", "level": "must-not", "rationale": "Semantic tokens are tuned for contrast within one mode. Mixing modes breaks those tuned pairings unpredictably." }, { "guidance": "Applying dark theme only to part of a page while the rest remains light.", "rationale": "Instead: Use inline theme switching for isolated components; apply the theme at the root for global coverage.", "level": "must-not" } ] } ] }, { "kind": "foundation", "identifier": "color", "name": "Color", "description": "The color system governing palette usage, semantic mapping, and accessibility.", "metadata": { "status": "stable", "tags": [ "color", "palette", "semantic", "contrast", "accessibility", "background", "text", "theme" ] }, "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "purpose": "Document semantic color tokens and enforce their consistent, accessible application across surfaces, text, and interactive states.", "items": [ { "description": "Color governs palette and semantic role mapping; theme entities govern how the palette shifts per mode.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "dark" } } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Do not hard-code hex values; reference semantic color tokens instead.", "level": "must-not", "rationale": "Hard-coded values escape theming, dark mode, and audits — the palette can no longer change in one place." }, { "guidance": "Maintain WCAG AA contrast for text and interactive elements on every surface.", "level": "must", "rationale": "AA is the system's compliance floor: 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text and interactive components." } ] } ] }, { "kind": "pattern", "identifier": "form-validation", "name": "Form Validation", "description": "Validates user input and communicates errors on form submission.", "metadata": { "status": "stable", "tags": [ "error", "validation", "form", "feedback", "inline-error", "error-summary", "alert" ] }, "agentDocumentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "purpose": "Guide multi-component composition for surfacing, communicating, and resolving form validation errors.", "items": [ { "description": "Use form-validation for input errors the user can fix at the field; use alert for system or status messages not tied to a field.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "alert" } }, { "description": "Use form-validation to communicate failure; success confirmation belongs to a toast, which never carries errors.", "stance": "discouraged", "alternative": { "identifier": "toast" } } ] }, { "kind": "guidelines", "items": [ { "guidance": "Always include both an inline field error and a summary when multiple fields fail.", "level": "must", "rationale": "The summary gives screen reader users one announcement point and an error count; inline errors give sighted users fix-it-here proximity. Each alone strands one audience." }, { "guidance": "Do not use toast notifications as the sole error communication for form validation.", "level": "must-not", "rationale": "Toasts dismiss themselves and are not tied to the failing field. Once gone, the user has no path back to what went wrong." }, { "guidance": "Do not show errors only on submit with no inline field feedback.", "rationale": "Users fix problems fastest at the field. Show inline errors on blur and collect them in a summary on submit.", "level": "must-not" } ] } ], "documentBlocks": [ { "kind": "use-cases", "items": [ { "description": "When a form submission can fail per field and the user must fix specific inputs before continuing.", "stance": "recommended" } ] } ] } ] } ] }
Validate your document

DSDS ships with a JSON Schema for validating your documents.

Using the bundled schema

Add $schema to get editor autocompletion and inline validation:

{ "$schema": "https://designsystemdocspec.org/v0.15.1/dsds.bundled.schema.json", "dsdsVersion": "0.15.1", "entity": { "kind": "component", "identifier": "my-component", "name": "My Component", "description": "What this component is and does." } } Using the CLI # Clone the repo git clone https://github.com/somerandomdude/design-system-documentation-schema.git cd design-system-documentation-schema npm install # Validate the built-in examples npm run validate # Validate your own file npx ajv validate -s spec/schema/dsds.bundled.schema.json -d my-system.dsds.json
Next steps

You've seen the basics. Here's where to go deeper.

ResourceDescription
Full SpecComplete schema reference for every field and constraint
Interactive SamplesSide-by-side JSON ↔ rendered documentation for real-world entities
JSON SchemaThe raw .schema.json files — use for editor autocompletion
Example FilesComplete, valid example documents for every entity and guideline type
GitHub DiscussionsAsk questions, share ideas, propose changes

Getting started recipe:

  1. Copy the minimal document above
  2. Replace the example entities with your own design system's components and tokens
  3. Add documentBlocks entries to each entity as your docs grow
  4. Validate with npm run validate to catch schema errors early

Design System Documentation Spec (DSDS) 0.15.1 — Draft Specification

GitHub
